saturation/rounding, so a `fold` sum is bit-identical only under a defined order; the engine
guarantees left-to-right, never tree-reduced or parallelized.

```funpack
assert fold([1.0, -1.0], Fixed.MAX, fn(acc, x) { return acc + x }) == Fixed.MAX - 1.0
```

title: 11 ŌĆö Physics & collision
text:
The engine integrates motion, detects collisions, and resolves contacts; user code writes **intent**
on its own body and **reacts to contacts** as signals. The hard part ŌĆö a deterministic solver ŌĆö is a
**Tier-1 native** (Odin, [`09`](09-runtime.md)), audited bit-identical exactly as `extern fn sin`
is. The language stays a fold. `engine.physics` / `engine.physics3` are the canonical surface.

---

title: 1. Determinism stance
text:
- **Sim physics is a custom fixed-point solver** ŌĆö Tier-1 native, contracted bit-identical on every
  machine, with a **fixed timestep**, a **fixed solver-iteration count** (an AX6 runtime constant,
  never a knob), and **deterministic contact ordering**. Lockstep/replay over a physical world is
  bit-identical ŌĆö the netcode payoff, not a separate problem.
- **The solver is discrete fixed-step ŌĆö there is no continuous collision detection.** A fast body can
  tunnel through thin geometry within a single step; the solver makes no swept guarantee against it.
  The escape hatch is **raycast-based movement** ŌĆö a body that must be tunneling-proof advances by
  clamping its motion to the first hit of the engine's ray queries (┬¦2, Tier 1), never relying on the
  integrator alone.
- **Vendored float physics (Box2D/Chipmunk) is visual-only, never sim** ŌĆö it may drive cosmetic
  debris/cloth/ragdolls in a render stage, behind the fixed-vs-float boundary; reaching the float
  path from synced state is a compile error (the warranty gate, [`25`](25-netcode.md)).

title: 2. Three tiers, one surface
text:
Escalate only as needed; the tier is visible in the signature, never a mode flag.

| Tier | You do | Engine provides |
|---|---|---|
| **0 ŌĆö kinematic** | set `pos` yourself; query by hand (`solid_at`/`pairs`/`@spatial`) | nothing physics-specific |
| **1 ŌĆö collision queries** | take a `Physics` resource; `raycast`/`shapecast`/`overlap`/`nearest`; decide your own response | pure deterministic detection |
| **2 ŌĆö dynamics** | declare a `Body`; set velocity / apply impulse on **your own** blackboard; match `Contact` | integration + detection + **resolution** (the native solver) |

title: Tier 1 ŌĆö queries are pure functions
text:
The world arrives as an injected resource `Physics` (`Physics3` in 3D); taking it *is* the statement
"this behavior consults collision". `Physics.of([ŌĆ”])` is the test fixture (the `View.of`/`Nav.of`
pattern).

```funpack
fn raycast(self: Physics, from: Vec2, dir: Vec2, max: Fixed, mask: [Layer]) -> Option[RayHit]
```

| call | shape |
|---|---|
| `raycast(phys, from, dir, max, mask)` | `Option[RayHit]` |
| `shapecast(phys, shape, from, dir, max, mask)` | `Option[RayHit]` |
| `overlap(phys, shape, at, mask)` | `[Ref[Thing]]` |
| `nearest(phys, point, mask)` | `Option[Ref[Thing]]` |

`RayHit` is serializable `data { body: Ref[Thing], point: Vec2, normal: Vec2, t: Fixed }`; absence is
`Option`, forced by a `match`.

title: Tier 2 ŌĆö declare a body, write intent, match a contact
text:
A thing becomes a body by carrying a `body: Body` field; `pos`/`vel` are the reserved fields the
solver integrates.

```funpack
data Body {
  kind:        BodyKind          // Static | Dynamic | Kinematic
  shape:       Shape2            // Circle | Box | Capsule | Polygon | Segment   (Shape3 in 3D)
  mass:        Fixed = 1.0
  restitution: Fixed = 0.0
  friction:    Fixed = 0.5
  layer:       Layer
  mask:        [Layer]
  sensor:      Bool = false      // detects overlaps but is never resolved (triggers)
  impulse:     Vec2 = zero       // accumulated intent; the solver consumes and zeroes it
}
```

`Static` never moves (infinite mass); `Dynamic` is fully solved (the solver owns its `pos`/`vel`);
`Kinematic` is moved by your code (push dynamics, never pushed back). `Shape3` is the same shape a
`.fpm` `collide` proxy bakes ([`16`](16-modeling.md)) ŌĆö a model's collision hull *is* its body shape.

A behavior influences physics only by writing **its own** body: `body.apply_impulse(j)` /
`body.apply_force(f)` return a new `Body` with intent accumulated; direct control is `self with {
vel: ŌĆ” }`. No hidden accumulator, no call into the solver.

title: 3. The `physics:` stage ŌĆö resolution is the engine's
text:
A collision writes **both** bodies, which a behavior may never do. So resolution is **not a
behavior**: it is an **engine-closed stage**, `physics:`, the **sixth** stage kind alongside
Startup/Update/Render/Ui/Audio ([`06`](06-things-behaviors.md), [`07`](07-pipelines.md)). Its single
member is the engine battery `solve`:

```funpack
pipeline Platformer {
  control: [read_input, run, jump]   // behaviors write their own body's intent
  physics: solve                     // ENGINE: integrate ┬Ę detect ┬Ę resolve ┬Ę emit Contact
  hits:    [on_contact, on_trigger]  // behaviors consume the Contact / Trigger signals
  render:  [draw_world]
}
```

`solve` reads every thing carrying a `Body`, integrates the dynamic ones, detects collisions
(broad-phase via the `@spatial` index, narrow-phase per shape pair), resolves contacts, and writes
back `pos`/`vel` ŌĆö the engine acting as the **sole effectful boundary**. **Stage position is the
ordering**: intent before `solve`, reactions after.

title: 4. Contacts & triggers ŌĆö signals routed per participant
text:
`solve` publishes collisions as engine signals, **routed by the engine to each participating
instance and oriented so the receiver is the subject** (the normal points away from `self`). A
behavior `on T` consuming `[Contact]` sees only its own instance's contacts, already in its frame ŌĆö
**no `self.id` to fetch, no list to filter**.

```funpack
signal Contact { normal: Vec2, point: Vec2, impulse: Fixed }   // normal points away from self
signal Trigger {}                                              // overlapping a sensor; no resolution
```

A solid pair yields one `Contact` to each body; a sensor pair yields one `Trigger` to each
overlapping body. Because the **engine** emits them, they are an **optional inbound edge** (like
reading `Time`): leaving one unconsumed is *not* an effect-closure violation (that rule binds
*user*-emitted signals). A contact behavior is a pure, testable fold over plain values.

title: 5. Collision filtering ŌĆö a registry, not a knob
text:
A body carries a `layer` and a `mask`. Two bodies collide **iff each mask contains the other's
layer** (symmetric AND ŌĆö stated, no hidden matrix). `Layer` is a project-declared closed set, checked
like a `@gtag`; an unregistered layer is a compile error.

```funpack
enum Layer: CollisionLayer { World, Player, Enemy, Pickup, Projectile }
```

title: 6. No joints or constraints ŌĆö constraints are behaviors
text:
The engine ships **bodies, collision, and triggers** ŌĆö there are **no joint or constraint types**. A
constraint is an ordinary **behavior over bodies** ([`06`](06-things-behaviors.md)): a behavior reads
body state and writes its own body's intent each step (the ┬¦2 `apply_impulse`/`apply_force`/`vel`
surface), feeding the `physics:` stage like any other intent. Hinges, springs, distance constraints,
and the rest are gameplay folds, not engine primitives ŌĆö keeping the effectful boundary the single
`solve` battery (┬¦3).

title: 7. Scope
text:
A dynamic body whose motion falls below an **engine-fixed rest threshold sleeps deterministically** ŌĆö
dropped from integration and the active set until a contact or applied force wakes it. The threshold
is a **fixed engine constant**, identical on every machine and run, **not configurable** from sim
code ŌĆö a tunable threshold would let two runs diverge on the sleep/wake boundary. Sleeping/islands
are engine-internal and unobservable, so they stay bit-identical. Collision filtering is the per-body
`layer`/`mask` registry of ┬¦5. The 3D solver covers primitive shapes; concave-mesh bodies are out of
scope.

title: 12 ŌĆö Navigation
text:
Spatial navigation ŌĆö a *thing* finding its way through a level. (UI navigation, the menu focus
cursor, is unrelated and lives in [`21`](21-ui.md).) The thesis applied: **the engine does the
pathfinding; the agent writes boring glue that follows a path.** Search is rich and native; the
language stays a fold. `stdlib/engine/{nav,nav3}.fun` match this component.

---

title: 1. The graph is baked ŌĆö you never build it
text:
There is no `addNode`/`addEdge`/`buildGraph`. A nav graph is **derived at bake** from the level ŌĆö
tilemap solids in 2D ([`18`](18-tilemaps.md)), a walkable navmesh from geometry in 3D ŌĆö and carried
in the world like the spawn list and tile layer. The worst nav bug, a graph drifted out of sync with
its geometry, is **unrepresentable** because there is no second source of truth. Dynamic terrain
follows for free: `SetTile` re-derives the affected nav incrementally, so a `path()` issued the tick
after a wall falls routes through the gap.

The active graph arrives as an injected resource **`Nav`** (`Nav3` in 3D), like `Input`/`Time`/`Rng`;
taking `nav: Nav` *is* the statement "this behavior consults navigation". A `NavHandle` names a baked
layer (ground vs. flying) ŌĆö the escape hatch; the default is the one resource and no name.

The authored surface is the single-level graph plus the pure `path()` query ŌĆö one flat graph, one
query contract, nothing else. **Hierarchical decomposition** ŌĆö clustering a large map into a coarse
graph over fine sub-graphs to bound search cost ŌĆö is an *invisible engine implementation detail*
behind that same contract, never a language surface: the author writes `path()` against one graph
whether the engine searches it directly or through tiers, the decomposition never appears in authored
data, never changes a route's purity, and adds no level for behaviors to address.

title: 2. Path search is a pure function, not a job
text:
Path search is a **pure, total, deterministic extern**, never an async job ŌĆö an async job's completion
tick is frame-time dependent (kills replay/lockstep), it is not testable as a fold, and a request-
command-plus-result-signal is two pipeline edges to close:

```funpack
fn path(self: Nav, from: Vec2, to: Vec2) -> Result[Path, NavError]
```

Same inputs ŌćÆ bit-identical `Path` on every machine (fixed tie-break: lowest `f`, then stable cell
order; fixed-point costs). `Path` is serializable `data` (`{ steps: [Vec2], cost: Fixed }`) ŌĆö store
it on a blackboard and replay re-derives it. Errors are values you must match: `Unreachable`,
`OffNav` ŌĆö no silently-empty path.

An endpoint resolves to the walkable cell **containing** it ŌĆö the grid's half-open cell partition,
exact and deterministic ŌĆö so a thing paths from where it *stands*, not from a center it must first
teleport onto; the route's steps are still given in cell centers ([┬¦5](#5-scope)). `OffNav` means
the containing cell is not walkable space at all: inside a solid, or off the grid. Snapping such a
point onto walkable space is `nearest()`'s job ŌĆö an explicit authored move, never an implicit
`path()` courtesy ŌĆö which is exactly why both error values and the snap tool exist side by side.

There is **no engine-driven replan cadence** ŌĆö a hidden timer re-routing agents around moving
obstacles is exactly the hidden control flow AX1 forbids, and the engine owns no such loop.
Adaptation to a changed world is two explicit, authored moves: a nav-affecting `SetTile` re-derives
the graph so the *next* query reflects the new topology ([┬¦1](#1-the-graph-is-baked--you-never-build-it)),
and a behavior re-issues `path()` on *its own* logic ŌĆö a `Fixed` countdown reaching zero ([`13`](13-ai.md)),
a `[Damaged]`-style signal, or a target drifting off the cached route. An agent chasing a moving
obstacle re-paths because its own `match` arm calls `path()` again, never because the engine decided
the tick for it.

> **"But pathfinding is expensive."** That is the engine's problem, stated as a *contract* (pure
> deterministic function), not the agent's, stated as an API. The implementation dedups identical
> queries per stage and backs them with an incremental flow field, so the naive **repath-every-tick**
> is fast by construction, and many-things-one-goal (RTS/tower-defense) is a shared flow field the
> engine infers ŌĆö not a second API.

title: 3. The surface
text:
Global route, then local follow. Nothing else.

| call | shape | use |
|---|---|---|
| `path(nav, from, to)` | `Result[Path, NavError]` | the route |
| `advance(path, pos, arrive)` | `(Option[Vec2], Path)` | next waypoint + remaining route ŌĆö a fold |
| `los(nav, from, to)` | `Bool` | straight segment clear? (string-pull / shortcut) |
| `reachable(nav, from, to)` | `Bool` | route exists? (cheaper than `path`) |
| `nearest(nav, point)` | `Option[Vec2]` | snap an off-nav target onto walkable space |

**`los` reads occupancy, not the graph.** Line-of-sight is a geometric visibility test ŌĆö *does the
straight segment cross a solid?* ŌĆö and solidity lives in the level's occupancy (the tilemap's solids
in 2D ([`18`](18-tilemaps.md)), the baked solid geometry in 3D), the **same single source the nav
graph derives from** ([┬¦1](#1-the-graph-is-baked--you-never-build-it)). The graph is connectivity,
not visibility: a straight segment between two connected centers can cross a solid that is simply a
non-edge, and two unconnected centers can see each other across a chasm. So `los` answers over the
**current** occupancy ŌĆö like `path()` after a wall falls (┬¦1), a `SetTile` wall blocks sight from
the next tick ŌĆö and the baked nav carries **no second copy of the solids** to drift against.

The test is conservative and exact: the segment's **supercover** ŌĆö every cell whose closed cell box
the closed segment `from ŌåÆ to` intersects ŌĆö must contain no solid cell. A segment through a lattice
corner therefore checks both diagonal neighbors (no line-of-fire through a kissing-corner seam), a
segment grazing a wall's face reads blocked, and `from == to` is `true` iff the containing cell is
clear. Tile-less cells and the out-of-grid void block nothing ŌĆö solidity is a property of a tile,
and you can *see* across a chasm you cannot *walk*: `los` is sight, `reachable` is feet.

`nav3` is the `Vec3`/navmesh twin ŌĆö identical names with a `3` suffix. The agent's glue is a pure
fold, tested with `Nav.of(route)`:

```funpack
behavior chase on Hunter {
  fn step(self: Hunter, nav: Nav, players: View[Player]) -> Hunter {
    let goal = nearest_player(self.pos, players)
    let route = match nav.path(self.pos, goal) {
      Result::Ok(p)  => p
      Result::Err(_) => self.path        // hold the last good route
    }
    return match route.advance(self.pos, ARRIVE) {
      (Option::Some(wp), rest) => self with { pos: step_to(self.pos, wp, SPEED), path: rest }
      (Option::None,     _)    => self
    }
  }
}
```

title: 4. Opinionated stances & non-features
text:
- **One algorithm, zero runtime knobs** ŌĆö exactly one `path()`; no A*-vs-Dijkstra, no heuristic
  weight, no diagonal toggle. Movement/diagonal/cost rules are a **bake-time** property of the level's
  nav, not a runtime argument.
- **Errors are values you must handle**; **two tiers one surface** (route + follow), with **no
  steering zoo** (seek/flee/wander/separation are boring glue over the `View[T]` neighbor join, not an
  engine library); **cost is a contract, not an API**.
- **No flow-field surface** ŌĆö the pure `path()` query is the *one* pathing model; a flow field is an
  engine implementation detail behind that contract (the shared structure ┬¦2 infers for
  many-things-one-goal), never a second authored pathing primitive an author requests. **No
  per-faction filter argument** (a baked nav layer selected by a `NavHandle`, not a runtime `mask:`);
  **no async/streaming search**.

title: 5. Scope
text:
A grid `Path` is given in cell centers; the raw `Cell` index is not exposed. Pathing is single-level;
routes do not cross streamed-chunk portals.

title: 13 ŌĆö AI, timing & sequencing
text:
Navigation ships pathfinding and stops; this component covers *decisions* ŌĆö when to chase, when to
give up, how a cutscene plays, how a cooldown works. The answer is the project's recurring one:
**the primitives already exist; AI is boring glue, not a new engine subsystem.** A behavior is a pure
transition over a blackboard ([`06`](06-things-behaviors.md)) ŌĆö which is exactly a state machine, a
timer, and a sequencer, with no new construct.

> **Thesis:** a state machine is a blackboard `enum` + an exhaustive `match`; a timer is a `Fixed`
> countdown folded by `Time`; a scripted sequence is a `Timeline` value advanced each tick. All three
> are plain data and pure transitions ŌĆö deterministic, serializable, unit-testable as folds, with no
> async, no coroutine, no decision DSL.

The hardest prior to resist is **async** ("wait N seconds, then act", `await`, a yielding coroutine):
hidden control flow and nondeterminism, the exact AX1 enemy. funpack replaces *waiting* with *state
advanced by the fixed tick* ŌĆö you decrement a counter and `match` on it, never await.

---

title: 1. Decisions ŌĆö an `enum` + an exhaustive `match`
2. Timing ŌĆö a countdown is a field, not a scheduler
3. Sequencing ŌĆö a `Timeline` is data, advanced each tick
text:
The linear/scripted complement to the branching FSM (a cutscene, a spawn wave, an attack combo).
`engine.seq` ships a `Timeline`, the sequencing twin of `nav.path` ŌĆö the engine owns ordering and the
elapsed-this-tick window; the body is a pure fold.

```funpack
data Step[A]     { at: Fixed, action: A }
data Timeline[A] { steps: [Step[A]], clock: Fixed = 0.0 }
fn advance(tl: Timeline[A], dt: Fixed) -> (Timeline[A], [A])   // actions whose time elapsed this window, in order
```

It is plain `data`, so a sequence is serializable ŌĆö a save mid-cutscene resumes at the same step and
a replay re-fires identical actions on identical ticks. `Timeline[A]` is a **reserved stdlib generic**
parameterized by the game's own action type (user code authors no generics). A short 2ŌĆō3 step beat is
just a cursor (`step: Int`, `step_t: Fixed`) `match`ed in a behavior; reach for `Timeline` when the
steps are many and data-shaped. **FSM for branching, Timeline for linear** ŌĆö one obvious tool per
shape.

title: 4. Selection & perception are pure folds
text:
- **Selection / utility** ŌĆö "the best target" is a `min_by_key`/`fold` scoring fold over a `View`
  (like arena's `nearest_player`). Utility AI is *scoring functions*, not an engine: there is no
  built-in subsystem that enumerates candidate actions and picks the highest-scoring one.
- **Perception** ŌĆö "can I see the player" is a pure predicate (a range/cone test, or `nav.los`); it
  takes a `View` and returns a value, so taking it *is* the statement that this behavior senses.

Neither **utility AI** nor **GOAP** ships as a built-in ŌĆö both are hidden decision machinery
competing with the authored `match`, the exact second decision structure ┬¦1 refuses. The shipped AI
surface is exactly three things: the blackboard `enum` matched exhaustively (the FSM), the `Fixed`
countdown ([┬¦2](#2-timing--a-countdown-is-a-field-not-a-scheduler)), and `Timeline` sequencing
([┬¦3](#3-sequencing--a-timeline-is-data-advanced-each-tick)). A **planner** ŌĆö utility scoring over
weighted considerations, or a GOAP-style operator search toward a goal ŌĆö is the **`extern` escape
hatch**: a library a behavior calls, which reads the blackboard, returns a chosen mode, and lets the
same exhaustive `match` dispatch on it. The planner lives *outside* the engine and writes back
through ordinary blackboard mutation; it is never a shipped engine subsystem.

title: 5. Determinism & rejected priors
text:
The whole AI is a pure fold over blackboard state ŌĆö `think`/`patrol`/`search`/`run_waves` test by
exact equality with a constructed `View` and `Time.at(dt)`, no world. Synced blackboards reproduce
identical AI **with no AI-specific network messages**, and a failing tick becomes a permanent
regression via `capture_test` ([`28`](28-introspection.md)).

**Rejected** (each breaks an axiom): async/`await`/coroutines/`yield`; a behavior-tree/GOAP engine as
a built-in (a second decision structure ŌĆö FSM + sub-pipelines cover the common case; a planner is an
escape hatch); wall-clock timers / `after(seconds, callback)`; `while`/unbounded loops in a decision.

AI state is a bare blackboard `enum` matched exhaustively ŌĆö there is no declared transition graph,
no `machine`/`state` keyword, no behavior-tree/GOAP/utility-curve battery. `Timeline` carries linear
beats; branching is the FSM. A planner is the `extern` escape hatch.

title: 14 ŌĆö Project structure & config (`.fcfg`)
text:
funpack enforces a **single** project layout ŌĆö there is no alternative arrangement and no override
(an "override the layout" flag is itself the config drift [`01`](01-axioms.md) P3 bans). The compiler
errors on a malformed tree as it errors on a malformed function. The layout splits along the
determinism boundary at the **filename**: `.fun` is the pure `source ŌåÆ artifact` input; **`.fcfg`**
is configuration the *impure* consumers (runtime, build driver) read.

---

title: 1. The tree
text:
```
proj/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ funpack_configs/          authored, committed, formatter-owned
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ project.fcfg          identity: name, version
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ entrypoints.fcfg      root pipeline Ōåö tick Ōåö bindings Ōåö net, per target
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ builds.fcfg           emit targets: platform (presentation) only
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ tags.fcfg             the @gtag registry
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ src/       *.fun          gameplay (tests inline)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ models/    *.fpm          bake-time rigs/models               ([`16`](16-modeling.md))
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ levels/    *.flvl         flat-text levels                    ([`17`](17-levels.md))
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ ui/        *.fui          UI templates                        ([`21`](21-ui.md))
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ assets/    *.manifest + sources (*.atlas, *.tiles, raw)       ([`18`](18-tilemaps.md), [`19`](19-assets.md))
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ gen/       *.gen.fun      compiler-owned, gitignored, rebuilt
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ .funpack/                 index + build products ŌĆö derived, gitignored
```

Module names derive from file paths ŌĆö the directory is the namespace, **no `module` declaration**
([`15`](15-modules.md)). The **capability set is derived, never declared** (┬¦4): a subsystem
directory present-but-empty ŌćÆ the feature is off; present-and-non-empty ŌćÆ the feature is on and the
matching `gen/` seam is expected. `gen/` and `.funpack/` are derived, gitignored, rebuilt on demand;
`funpack_configs/` is authored and committed.

title: 2. Why config is `.fcfg`, not `.fun`
text:
Configuration holds wiring for impure consumers (`tick`/`bindings`/`net` ŌåÆ runtime;
`platform` ŌåÆ build driver; project facts ŌåÆ the index) ŌĆö none of it is `source ŌåÆ artifact`. `.fcfg`
keeps the pure/impure split visible in the filesystem. Its grammar is deliberately **smaller** than
`.fun`'s: typed `key = value` plus `use mod.{ŌĆ”}` references, a fixed set of declaration kinds, and
**no expressions, no control flow, no behaviors**. The lexical tells ŌĆö `=` not `:`, `use` not
`import` ŌĆö signal "config, not logic" on sight. It inherits the **no-free-comment** discipline
(P6): `@doc` describes a declaration, `@stub`/`@todo` anchor an undecided or debt-carrying value.

title: 3. `.fcfg` is import-terminal and emits no seam
text:
`.fcfg` names source; source never names it. It feeds the **authored** fields of the index
contract's `project` record (`entrypoints`, `builds`, `tag_registry`); the derived fields
(`capabilities`, `pipeline_flattened`, `gate_results`) project from source. It is the **one** authoring
format that bakes **no** `.gen.fun` seam ŌĆö and three properties depend on that:

- the pipeline stays a **pure schedule** (wiring isn't read back into source);
- behaviors stay **harness-free testable** (no behavior reads a feature handle);
- **one-way data flow** (no `source ŌåÆ config` arrow, no cycle).

The asymmetry with assets is the line: **assets get a typed seam because gameplay consumes them
every tick**; **config is deployment shape consumed by tools**, never per-tick content.

title: 4. The files
text:
- **`project.fcfg`** ŌĆö minimal project identity; the block label **is** the project name and it
  carries `version`: `project pong { version = "0.1.0" }`. It is the **package identity at the
  package boundary** ([`15`](15-modules.md)) ŌĆö that is what the `name` earns its keep as.
- **`entrypoints.fcfg`** ŌĆö each root pipeline with its lifted wiring. Multiple entrypoints are legal
  as **distinct sims** (a benchmark, a tutorial), **not** client/server (one entrypoint projected by
  realm). Selection is inferred (┬¦6).
  ```
  use pong.{Pong, bindings}
  entrypoint main { pipeline = Pong, tick = 60hz, logical = 160x120, bindings = bindings }
  ```
  `logical = WxH` is the **required** fixed logical draw space ([`20`](20-render.md) ┬¦3) in positive
  integer world units ŌĆö the extent the engine scales and letterboxes to the window. It is declared
  here, beside `tick`, because it is runtime wiring the pipeline carries no configuration for
  ([`07`](07-pipelines.md) ┬¦1); the sim's own constants (Pong's `BOARD`) remain ordinary game code,
  and the engine letterboxes to the *declared* extent.
- **`builds.fcfg`** ŌĆö the **presentation** platform targets (`desktop`, `wasm`) and nothing else.
  **No `realm` field**: the server/client split is derived from source realm + switched on by `net:`
  in the entrypoint ([`25`](25-netcode.md)).
- **`tags.fcfg`** ŌĆö the `@gtag` registry ([`05`](05-directives.md)); an unregistered tag is a
  compile error.

**The capability set is derived, never declared** ŌĆö there is no features config file. Each engine
battery switches on from its backing source:

- a non-empty `ui/` ŌćÆ **UI**;
- a non-empty `models/` ŌćÆ **modeling**;
- a `net:` in an entrypoint ŌćÆ **netcode** ([`25`](25-netcode.md));
- an `@expose` declaration ŌćÆ **modding** ([`27`](27-modding.md));
- an `audio:` stage ŌćÆ **audio** ([`22`](22-audio.md)).

Core subsystems (render/input/state) are always on. A declared import without its backing directory
is a normal resolution error, so a stray capability cannot be wired without its source present.

title: 5. Capability variation is structural, never a config read
text:
A behavior that branches on an active feature is the anti-pattern. Variation is by **composition**:
a feature that is off has its stage simply **not composed** into the entrypoint's pipeline (you add
a stage; you never write `if feature.audio`); server/client variation is realm projection making the
wrong-realm code **unnameable** ([`25`](25-netcode.md)) ŌĆö a closure, not a conditional.

title: 6. Platforms, realms, and the CLI
text:
Platforms **factor** with realm rather than multiplying: a declared platform always targets the
**client/standalone** artifact; the **server** is never a declared platform ŌĆö under `net:
authoritative` it is emitted **once, headless, by construction** (it has no `Draw` consumer). The
emitted set is `|platforms|` clients + (`authoritative` ? 1 server : 0). OS/arch triples and
server-only builds are downstream build-driver / CLI concerns, not config.

The invocation **selects among committed declarations** and supplies **runtime-only deploy params**;
it never **synthesizes** a value that enters the artifact (`funpack run [name]`, `funpack build
--target wasm`, `funpack serve --port 7777`). Entrypoint selection is **inferred** ŌĆö one entrypoint
is the implicit default; multiple force a named pick (no `default =` field, no first-wins).
`funpack check` adjudicates this same tree ŌĆö the full checked pipeline with **no product written**
([`29`](29-architecture-governance.md) ┬¦3) ŌĆö so a malformed tree refuses under `check` exactly as it
refuses under `build`.

title: 7. Project facts, not knobs
text:
`funpack_configs` holds **project facts** (which pipeline is the root, the tick rate, which platforms
ship) ŌĆö irreducibly per-game, with no universal default ŌĆö categorically distinct from P5's banned
**knobs** (gate budgets, per-site waivers, `holes = allow`). The active battery set is **not** a
project fact: it is derived from source (┬¦4). `devŌåörelease` stays the compiler-owned `--release`
**mode**, never a config field.

title: 8. File minimalism
text: `name` is retained in `project.fcfg` as the package identity ([`15`](15-modules.md)).

title: 15 ŌĆö Modules & visibility
text:
A module's name **is its path**. Nothing declares it ŌĆö there is **no `module` keyword**. A `.fun`
file's module is its location under the source root: directory segments dotted, filename as the leaf.
This is the same derive-don't-declare discipline as realm, pipeline wiring, and entrypoint selection
ŌĆö the filesystem is the single source of truth, and a redundant `module X` line that could drift from
its path does not exist to get wrong.

---

title: 1. The rule
text:
| File (under the source root) | Module |
|---|---|
| `src/pong.fun` | `pong` |
| `src/combat/melee.fun` | `combat.melee` |
| `src/combat.fun` | `combat` |
| `stdlib/engine/math.fun` | `engine.math` |

- **A directory is a namespace.** `src/combat/` is the `combat` namespace.
- **An optional sibling `<name>.fun` carries that namespace's own declarations** (`src/combat.fun`
  *is* module `combat`; `src/combat/melee.fun` is `combat.melee`). The two coexist (Rust-2018 style);
  there is **no `mod.fun` index file**.
- **A directory with no sibling file is a pure namespace** (a prefix only; `import engine.{ŌĆ”}` names
  nothing ŌĆö there is no `engine.fun`).

title: 2. The module doc
text:
The module is implicit, so its documentation attaches **by position**: a `@doc` that is the first
item in a file ŌĆö before any `import` or declaration ŌĆö documents the file's module (a Rust `//!` /
Python module-docstring, in directive grammar). A pure-namespace directory has no module doc.

title: 3. Imports are absolute
text:
Rooted at the namespace root, always. There are **no relative imports** ŌĆö no `self`/`super`/`../`
(a relative import means a different module in different files, the exact ambiguity P-roots forbid).

```
import engine.math                          // whole module
import engine.math.max                       // one item
import engine.math.{Fixed, Vec2, clamp}      // selected items
```

`.fcfg`'s `use` resolves the same paths ([`14`](14-project-config.md)).

title: 4. Visibility ŌĆö two boundaries
text:
| Boundary | Rule |
|---|---|
| **within a project** (module ŌåÆ sibling module) | public-by-default, no markers |
| **across a package edge** (package ŌåÆ consumer/mod) | importable **iff `@expose`d**; the rest is package-private |

**Within a project**, every top-level declaration is importable. There is **no `pub` modifier** and
no private declarations. Encapsulation is a property of **module granularity** ŌĆö put a helper you
don't want widely used in its own narrow module ŌĆö not a per-declaration knob. One reason per root: P5
(a visibility modifier is a per-site knob); P7 (the reuse/duplication gate *wants* every helper
visible to the index, so a reimplementation is caught); P2 (one fewer concept). `funpack warden find`
noise from internal helpers is mitigated by `@gtag`/`@doc` ranking (an untagged, undoc'd helper ranks
low).

**Across a package edge**, a dependency exposes a deliberate API: an item is importable by a consumer
only if marked `@expose` ([`05`](05-directives.md)), and the exposed set generates the package's
`<name>.api.gen.fun` contract. This is the one place a visibility marker appears ŌĆö the external
contract is an irreducible design act with no universal answer, not a knob ŌĆö and the same `@expose`
surface is what a mod is confined to. See [`30`](30-packages.md) ┬¦6 and [`27`](27-modding.md).

title: 5. The package boundary
text:
The **project name is not a namespace prefix.** Within a project, modules root at the source root
unprefixed (`pong`, `combat.melee`). The project name becomes a root namespace **only across the
package boundary** ŌĆö an external project `foo` pulled in via the curated-package escape hatch has
modules `foo.*` (the Rust crate-name rule). The **stdlib is the built-in package**, occupying the
reserved root **`engine`**; a user `src/engine/` is a name-collision **compile error** (reserved
roots are unshadowable). This is what earns `project.fcfg`'s `name` its keep.

title: 6. Generated seams
text:
A generated seam's module is its **source filename**, placed in the **root namespace** ŌĆö `ui/hud.fui`
generates module `hud`, `models/krognid.fpm` generates module `krognid`. `.gen.fun` is a
compiler-owned filename marker, **not** a namespace segment, so a seam imports as if hand-authored ŌĆö
invisible at the import site. Two sources producing the **same module name is a compile error**.

title: 7. Reserved roots
text:
`engine` is the **single** reserved root namespace ŌĆö the built-in stdlib package. The reserved set is
**closed and compiler-fixed** (there is no extensible registry); a user module path under `engine.*`
is a **compile error** ([`14`](14-project-config.md)). A module's leading `@doc` is permitted on the
public surface.

title: 16 ŌĆö Modeling DSL (`.fpm`)
text:
funpack deliberately has **two languages**: the **modeling DSL** (`.fpm`) ŌĆö imperative, float-tolerant,
scripty (OpenSCAD/Python feel), running **at bake time only** ŌĆö and the **`.fun` core** ŌĆö pure,
fixed-point, gated, which never sees a `.fpm` and consumes only the generated typed seam. The seam is
the load-bearing idea: **script ŌåÆ model ŌåÆ generated references + wiring**, stable enough that a
procedural prototype is **replaced incrementally** by hand-authored assets without touching game code.

title: 1. Why a second language doesn't break the axioms
text:
- **P3** ŌĆö modeling *is* an asset-pipeline stage; its expressiveness is a property of engine
  batteries (CSG, sweeps, materials), not user abstraction.
- **R1** ŌĆö the **file type is the disambiguating token**: `.fpm` is unambiguously bake-time/float/
  imperative, `.fun` unambiguously sim-time/fixed-point/pure. No single source mixes the regimes.
- **P4** ŌĆö modeling needs only **build** determinism (same script + inputs ŌåÆ identical hashed asset;
  seeded noise, no wall-clock); it is exempt from **simulation** determinism because its output is
  baked and content-hashed, never re-evaluated in the hot path.

`.fpm` reuses the core token priors (`fn`, `let`, `for x in`, `{ ŌĆ” }`, `return`) and **allows
`//` comments**; it diverges only where bake-time freedom earns it ŌĆö **local mutation, accumulating
loops, and float arithmetic are legal**, because none survives into the sim. Concretely: a local
**reassignment** is `name = expr` (the l-value is a bound local or a dotted path into one), and an
**accumulating loop** is `for x in <iterable> { ŌĆ” }` over a list or an integer range `a..b`. Both are
bake-time only and have no `.fun` counterpart ŌĆö the sim has neither mutation nor loops
([`02`](02-language-core.md) ┬¦6).

title: 2. The `model` / `rig` vocabulary
text:
```
model Table {
  param width: Length = 120
  fn leg() -> Solid { return box(6, 6, height) }
  emit     union(slab, legs)                  // render geometry
  anchor   seat_top = slab.face("top").center // a named point
  socket   cup      = seat_top.offset(z: 2)   // an attach point
  material body     = pbr(color: oak, rough: 0.6)
  collide  proxy    = box(width, depth, height) // a sim-side hull
}
```

| Keyword | Purpose | Generates |
|---|---|---|
| `param` | a tunable knob + default | a field on the params `data` |
| `emit` | the render geometry (`Solid`) | a content-hashed `MeshHandle` |
| `anchor` | a named point/plane | an `Anchors` entry, queried semantically |
| `socket` | an anchor intended as an attach point | an `Anchors` socket entry |
| `material` | an appearance slot (engine PBR; no user shaders) | a material binding |
| `collide` | a sim-side collision proxy | a fixed-point `Shape3` ([`11`](11-physics.md)) |

The geometry algebra is engine-provided and **boring on purpose**: primitives (`box`, `sphere`,
`cyl`, `capsule`), booleans (`union`, `difference`, `intersect`), transforms (`.at`, `.rotate`,
`.scale`, `.up`/`.down`), and the 2DŌåö3D bridge (`extrude`, `revolve`, `loft` over a `Sketch`). CSG
booleans ride the OpenSCAD prior. Half-edge editing, SDF authoring, and node graphs are rejected.

A **`rig`** block adds character authoring: `skeleton: <topology>` (a stdlib topology or inline
tree), `part <name> at BONE = <mesh>` (the part's modeled origin **is** that bone's joint ŌĆö a checked
pivot, not a comment), `mirror L -> R` (model one side, generate the other), `clearance N` (a
warn-level minimum joint gap).

title: 3. Gates
text:
`.fpm` carries **soft** structural guidance (a model past a size threshold *should* decompose into
sub-`model`s / helper `fn`s ŌĆö the bake **warns**) and **hard** geometry-invariant gates (non-manifold,
self-intersecting, zero-volume, or over-budget meshes **fail** the bake ŌĆö the modeling analogue of
the P5 quality gate).

title: 4. The generated interface (`*.gen.fun`)
5. Incremental replacement = `@stub` for assets
text:
The generated interface is a **stable typed seam** ŌĆö the asset-world analogue of a typed hole. The
procedural script is merely the *first* implementation behind it; a hand-authored mesh is the second.
The backing is a one-line manifest swap, game code untouched:

```
backing: procedural("table.fpm")                                 // prototype
// backing: asset("assets/table.glb", anchors: "table.anchors")  // later, hand-made
```

Swapping rebinds the same anchors/sockets/params onto the imported mesh (the importer requires the
named anchors to be placed). The same flow serves 2D drawings (a `Sketch`/sprite behind the seam).

title: 6. Self-review
text:
Two layers, neither asking an agent to read triangle soup: the **script** (small, high-prior, named
intermediates, timeless `@doc`) and the **generated seam** carrying a **digest** ŌĆö a compact
deterministic fingerprint diffed in place of geometry:

```funpack
data Digest { bbox: Aabb, tris: Int, watertight: Bool, components: Int, symmetry: [Plane], anchors: [Anchor] }
```

Tests assert on the digest and anchors, not coordinates (`d.components == 1`, `d.anchors.find("seat_top").z == 74.0`).
**Golden snapshots** are legal because a fixed camera + lights render deterministically ŌĆö a stored
silhouette/perceptual hash is a stable, reviewable signal feeding the same writeŌåÆcheckŌåÆfix loop.

title: 7. Rigs, parts & animation
text:
A skeleton is three things funpack already has, composed: **parts** (the `.fpm` DSL), a **skeleton**
(engine value), and **pose generators** (pure `fn`s ŌĆö the functional core applied to bones).

- **`Skeleton`** is engine-provided (`stdlib/engine/anim.fun` ŌĆö an opaque `extern type`; modeling.md's
  `{bones, parents, rest, slots}` is its conceptual shape), built via `Skeleton.humanoid()` /
  `quadruped()` / `robot()` or an inline `rig` tree whose node grammar is the engine `Skeleton`
  builder surface (engine-defined). The authored form in `skeleton:` is a **named topology**
  (`skeleton: humanoid`); the inline tree is the rarely-needed engine-defined escape.
- **`Pose`** is a sparse `bone -> Transform` map; a **pose generator** is a pure `fn` returning only
  the bones it drives: `Pose.empty().set(Bone::LUpperLeg, rot_x(s))ŌĆ”`. Composition is two engine
  primitives ŌĆö `Pose.blend(a, b, w)` (per-bone lerp/slerp) and `Pose.layer(base, overlay)` (overlay
  wins per bone). Both compose over the **union** of the two poses' driven bones; a bone one pose
  drives and the other omits blends (or layers) against the other pose's **rest (identity) transform**
  ŌĆö the same absent-bone default `Pose.get` returns for an undriven bone, in **both directions**.
- **Layering *is* pipeline order** ŌĆö a pose sub-pipeline lists generators top-to-bottom and *position
  is the layer* (`pose: [pose_idle, pose_walk, pose_carry]`); no magic-number layers. The render stage
  consumes the composed pose via `Draw3::Rigged{ skeleton, parts, pose, at }` ([`20`](20-render.md)).
- **Determinism boundary, per bone:** a **gameplay-observable** bone (a hand carrying a weapon
  hitbox, a foot driving sim-read IK) **must** be fixed-point in a sim stage and replay-safe (needs
  fixed-point `sin`/`slerp`, [`10`](10-numerics.md)); **purely cosmetic** secondary motion (breathing,
  jiggle) may run float in the render stage.

Validation gates (extending P5 to rigs): part origin == declared bone pivot (error); every bound slot
has a mesh (error); mirrored side declared not duplicated (error); joint clearance Ōēź `clearance`
(warning); rest-pose manifold/bounds (digest).

title: 8. Where it runs
text:
Bake time (`.fpm` eval, CSG/meshing, hashing, `.gen.fun` emission ŌĆö float here); sim time (only the
generated seam ŌĆö fixed-point params, `Shape3`, anchor math); render time (`[Draw3]`). Modeling is
**strictly bake-time**: there is **no runtime re-bake** and **no runtime-procedural geometry**
(`fn(state) -> Solid` per tick is **excluded**) ŌĆö either would pull meshing into the deterministic sim,
and the generated seam is content-hashed once and never re-evaluated in the hot path (┬¦4).

**Level of detail is an invisible engine/bake concern, not an authoring surface.** LOD generation ŌĆö
decimated rungs feeding distance-selected rendering ŌĆö is an implementation detail behind the same
content-hashed `MeshHandle` the seam already exposes; a model declares one `emit` and the bake/engine
produce and select LODs transparently. There is no LOD authoring keyword, no per-rung script, and the
sim never observes which rung renders ([`20`](20-render.md)).

title: 17 ŌĆö Levels (`.flvl`)
text:
There is no scene editor ŌĆö funpack is flat text. A level is, in the runtime model, just an **initial
world**: placed `thing`s with their blackboard params and the references between them ŌĆö the same
thing `setup() -> [Spawn]` builds by hand and the same shape a save deserializes to
([`08`](08-state.md)). A `.flvl` bakes to a generated `*.gen.fun` seam the game consumes, and is
**incrementally replaceable**. One format serves 2D and 3D; only the coordinate arity changes.

> **Thesis:** a level is an initial snapshot. The `.flvl` is ergonomic authoring sugar (anchors,
> loops, prefabs, references-by-name) that bakes to a deterministic spawn list and a typed seam; that
> baked output is the canonical world-data a save also produces.

The problem it solves is **spatial reasoning without a viewport**: an LLM can't see that `(152, 60)`
is "the right paddle," so every choice replaces absolute coordinates with names, relationships,
repetition, and symmetry.

title: 1. The `.flvl` surface
text:
```
level Arena 2d {
  bounds (0, 0) (160, 120)
  things  arena_world                 // the schema module whose thing types this level places

  prefab Turret {
    place Base   base   at origin
    place Cannon cannon { rate: 2.0 } at base.offset(y: 6)
  }

  place Player hero  at center
  place Switch plate at center.offset(y: 40)
  place Door   exit  { gate: plate } at center.offset(y: -40)   // gate: Ref[Switch] resolved from `plate`
  for i in 0..5 { place Pillar at center.offset(x: -48 + i * 24, y: 0) }
  place Turret right_gun { cannon.rate: 4.0 } at right_edge.center.offset(x: -12)  // override nested by path
}
```

- **`place <Type> <name> { params } at <where> [facing <rot>]`** ŌĆö instantiates a `thing`, setting
  blackboard fields inline (typechecked against the thing's schema), under a stable **name** ŌĆö the
  level's anti-hallucination currency and what the seam exposes. `<name>` is optional for one-off
  scenery (anonymous instances don't appear in the seam).
- **`2d` / `3d`** is the header word: coordinates are `(x, y)` / `(x, y, z)`; `facing` is an angle /
  an orientation.

title: Reserved fields
text:
`at` and `facing` write ordinary blackboard fields (position is gameplay state, not a hidden engine
transform): **`at` writes `pos`** (`Vec2` in a 2d level, `Vec3` in 3d) and **`facing` writes
`facing`**. The *name*, not the type, is the contract ŌĆö a thing may also carry a `vel: Vec2`. A thing
placed with `at` **must** declare a matching `pos` of the level's arity (also the dimensionality
check); a spatial-less thing is placed without `at`.

title: Killing raw coordinates
text:
Positions resolve against things a model reasons about well: **bounds anchors** (`center`,
`left_edge`, `right_edge.center`, ŌĆ” with `.offset(x:, y:[, z:])`); **instance-relative** (`base.top`,
`left_of(door, 2)`, `above(table)`); **model sockets** (`table.socket("cup")`, baked by a `.fpm`);
**repetition** (`for i in 0..N`, `grid`/`row` helpers).

title: References by name ŌåÆ typed `Ref[T]`
text:
A param pointing at another thing is written as that thing's **name**; the bake resolves it to a typed
`Ref[T]` ([`08`](08-state.md)) and checks it. A nonexistent name, a duplicate name, or a target whose
type doesn't match the field's `Ref[T]` is a **compile error** ŌĆö dangling references are
unrepresentable.

title: Prefabs
text:
A named bundle of placements with its own local origin and names; placing it stamps the bundle ŌĆö
**data composition**, not user code. Prefabs **nest to arbitrary depth** (a prefab places other
prefabs, with no depth cap); each placement carries its own local origin and name, so a member at any
depth is addressed by its **dotted name-path** (`right_gun.cannon.rate`). Overrides are by dotted path
into the prefab's members (`{ cannon.rate: 4.0 }`) and apply at **any depth** the name-path reaches;
an override of a nonexistent field is a compile error. The bake resolves overrides **deterministically**
ŌĆö outer placement over nested default, declaration order ŌĆö so a stamp is a pure expansion of nested
data regardless of nesting depth.

title: 2. The generated seam
text:
Committed, formatter-canonical funpack: a deterministic spawn list plus a **typed symbol table** of
the level's named instances (a prefab instance expands to a small `data` of its members' `Ref`s):

```funpack
data Arena { hero: Ref[Player], plate: Ref[Switch], exit: Ref[Door], right_gun: ArenaTurret }
fn arena_spawns() -> [Spawn]        // deterministic, declaration order, prefabs/loops expanded in place
fn arena() -> Arena                 // the symbol table, valid once the level is loaded
```

**Stable ids by name:** a named instance's `Id` is derived from its level-qualified name, so its
`Ref` is constant across loads/saves/replays; anonymous scenery takes counter ids in declaration
order. The propagation property holds ŌĆö rename `plate` in the level and `Arena.plate` disappears,
so every reader stops compiling at exactly the spot to fix.

title: Module layering ŌĆö schema / seam / behavior
text:
A general 3-way split (required whenever a generated seam names your types ŌĆö levels, netcode, save,
modding), keeping the import graph **acyclic by construction**:

- **Schema module** ŌĆö only `thing`/`data`/`enum`/`signal`; imports engine types only
  (`arena_world`).
- **Generated seam** ŌĆö imports **schema modules only**; a `.gen.fun` importing a behavior module is a
  compile error (`arena`).
- **Behavior module** ŌĆö imports schema + seam; declares the `behavior`s and the `pipeline`
  (`arena_game`).

A small game with no type-referencing seam (Pong) needs no split.

title: 3. Levels are snapshots are saves
text:
The baked output is the **canonical world-data** ([`08`](08-state.md)): a **level** is an initial
snapshot (authored), a **save** is a runtime snapshot, a **replay seed** is the tick-0 snapshot. The
`.flvl` sugar is a **one-way** lowering to that flat data ŌĆö a running world dumps *back* to the flat
form (for a save, or for an agent to inspect via [`28`](28-introspection.md)), never back into the
sugar.

title: 4. Streaming, determinism & gates
text:
`include "town/market.flvl"` splits a big level across files; **chunks** stream via `Load{ level, at
}` / `Unload{ level }` commands over a content-hashed `LevelHandle` (the `[Spawn]`-class path,
`stdlib/engine/level.fun`). `Volume` ({`Rect`/`Box`/`Sphere`}) models regions/triggers. Spawn order is
declaration order (prefabs expanded in place); named ids are name-derived; coordinates are
fixed-point ŌĆö so a level loads bit-identically on every machine. Bake gates (P5): unresolved/duplicate
names, type-mismatched references, params/overrides not on the schema, an `at` without a matching
`pos`, placement outside `bounds`, a seam importing a behavior module, and a prefab member referenced
but not placed are all **compile errors**.

The `.flvl` lowering is one-way: on a snapshot dump, names survive; the sugar does not.

title: 18 ŌĆö Sprites & tilemaps
text:
2D content is two layers: **sprites** (the textured-quad draw primitive) and **tilemaps** (grids of
tiles that make up the environment). A tilemap is a **level layer** ([`17`](17-levels.md)), and its
authored form answers the level module's hardest problem head-on:

> **The grid is the viewport.** A tilemap is an **ASCII grid** the agent reads, edits, and diffs *as
> a picture*; the same grid both paints the environment and places the entities.

title: 1. Sprites
text:
A sprite is a region of a texture **atlas** drawn as a quad ŌĆö a `Draw` command, as data
([`20`](20-render.md)):

```funpack
Draw::Sprite{ atlas: assets.pickups, cell: "coin", at: self.pos, size: Vec2{x:8.0, y:8.0},
              tint: Color::White, flip: Flip::None, layer: 5 }
```

- **Atlas** ŌĆö a baked sprite sheet of named regions (`atlas("items") -> AtlasHandle`, an asset).
  Authored in an `.atlas` file: `atlas Pickups { image "pickups.png"; grid 8 8; cell coin at (0,0);
  clip spin cells ["coin","gem","key"] fps 8 }`.
- **Cell** ŌĆö a region id (`String`): a named region (`"coin"`) or a grid cell `atlas.cell(col, row)`.
  Names, not pixel rects ŌĆö legible and diffable (P7).
- **`layer: Int`** ŌĆö explicit back-to-front draw order, a stated number.
- **`flip`** ŌĆö `X`/`Y`/`XY` mirroring (the rig-mirror idea in 2D).

**Animation is a pure projection:** a named clip (ordered cells + rate); `atlas.frame(clip, t)` is
total and deterministic (fixed-point clock ŌćÆ replay-identical), so an animated sprite is unit-tested
by exact equality like any other render.

title: 2. Tilesets (`.tiles`)
3. Tilemaps ŌĆö the ASCII grid
text:
Authored inside a `level` as a layer: a **legend** mapping single characters to a *tile* (static
environment) or a *spawn* (an entity), and a **grid** of those characters.

```
tilemap floor cell 16 {            // layer `floor`; each cell is 16 logical units
  legend {
    '#' wall                       // a tile (static, solid)
    '.' floor                      // a tile (passable)
    'g' spawn Goblin               // an anonymous marker ŌĆö spawn a thing at this cell
    'P' spawn Player hero          // a named marker (must be unique)
    ' ' empty
  }
  grid """
    ################
    #..........g...#
    #...P.....####.#
    ################
  """
}
place Switch lever at cell(5, 3)   // the relational few use explicit place + the cell() anchor
place Door   exit  { gate: lever } at cell(11, 2)
```

The **grid** handles the spatial bulk (walls, floors, swarms of anonymous entities ŌĆö what the agent
reasons about as a picture); **`place`** handles the relational few (named, referenced, precisely
positioned), tied into the grid via the `cell(col, row)` anchor. A **named marker** must appear
exactly once (a duplicate is a bake error); anonymous markers repeat freely.

A legend's tile name resolves through the **project-global tile namespace**: every `.tiles`
tileset in the project contributes its tiles to one flat name set, the same bare-name style an
`atlas` reference uses ŌĆö a tilemap names no tileset. Two tilesets declaring the same tile name
is a bake error (one name, one tile), the duplicate-named-marker discipline applied to tiles.

It bakes to: a **tile layer** (a 2D array the engine renders **batched** and collides against ŌĆö you
never emit per-tile `Draw::Sprite`); a **`[Spawn]`** per marker at its cell center (row-major
declaration order ŌćÆ deterministic ids); and the level **seam** gains the named markers as `Ref`s and
the layer as a `TilemapHandle`.

title: 4. Collision, queries & dynamic tiles
text:
Behaviors query the baked layer through the `TilemapHandle` ŌĆö no per-tile entities, fixed-point and
replay-safe (`stdlib/engine/tilemap.fun`): `tile_at(cell) -> Option[String]`, `solid_at(cell) ->
Bool`, `cell_of(pos) -> Cell`, `center_of(cell) -> Vec2`; stable cell order makes every query
deterministic. A handle-touching behavior is tested with `TilemapHandle.of(cell_size, cells)` (the
`View.of`/`Nav.of` mold): the fixture seeds a small layer from `(cell, tile, solid)` rows in its
own grid-local space anchored at the origin ŌĆö no legend, no tileset ŌĆö and the four queries answer
over it; an unseeded cell reads `Option::None`/not-solid, never a fault. Destructible terrain is a command: a behavior returns `SetTile{ map, cell, tile }`
(the `[Spawn]`-class path), applied deterministically at tick end; render, collision, and the nav
graph ([`12`](12-navigation.md)) update from the same data. The rewritten cells are committed world
state, not bake decoration: across a hot-reload swap the live `SetTile` delta carries onto the new
bake ([`09`](09-runtime.md) ┬¦4), and across save/restore it rides the snapshot
([`24`](24-persistence.md) ┬¦1).

**Runtime generation ŌĆö the whole-layer twin.** `SetTile` *edits* a cell; a seeded behavior that
*builds* a layer (a procedural floor, a generated map) emits its bulk twin: `BuildLayer{ map, fill,
cells }` ŌĆö `fill` is the base tile every cell takes and `cells: [(Cell, String)]` the explicit
overrides ŌĆö applied atomically at tick end as one committed-state write that **replaces** the layer's
contents (not a delta accreted over prior edits). It is an ordinary pipeline behavior result threaded
with the seeded `Rng` ([`04`](04-effects.md) ┬¦1, [`26`](26-stdlib.md)) ŌĆö the engine compiles and runs
it like any behavior, so **generation never makes the runtime compile source** (the engine boundary,
[`01`](01-axioms.md)); per-floor entities are the ordinary `[Spawn]` path. Everything `SetTile`
guarantees holds: render, collision, and the nav graph ([`12`](12-navigation.md)) re-derive from the
new layer; the materialized layer is committed world state that carries across a hot-reload swap
([`09`](09-runtime.md) ┬¦4) and rides the save snapshot ([`24`](24-persistence.md) ┬¦1), bounded by the
layer's extent rather than an unbounded edit log. The layer's **extent and tileset are still declared
once by its authored `.flvl`** (the canvas); generation paints it. A level remains an initial snapshot
([`17`](17-levels.md) ┬¦3) ŌĆö a generated layer is runtime committed state, never `.flvl` sugar
re-evaluated live (the lowering is one-way). Variable per-floor extent is out of scope here: a built
layer fills its declared extent.

The integer-grid helpers (`stdlib/engine/grid.fun`): `Cell { x, y }`, `grid_cells(size: Cell) ->
[Cell]`, `neighbors(cell)`, `in_bounds(cell, size)`. *(Canonical: `grid_cells` takes a `Cell` and
returns the cells; the 3-argument mapper form `grid_cells(w, h, fn)` used by `examples/snake` is
non-idiomatic ŌĆö map over the cell list instead.)*

title: 5. 2D / 3D & determinism
text:
Textured tile *rendering* is 2D, but the **grid-with-legend authoring generalizes to 3D**: a marker
char places a **prefab** per cell (the classic blockout grid), with stacked `tilemap` layers giving
the third axis. Spawn/render order is row-major then explicit `place`s in declaration order
(bit-identical load). Bake gates (P5): a char not in the legend, a non-rectangular grid, a tile name
not in the tileset, a duplicate named marker, a marker without a `pos` of the level's arity, an
unresolved reference, or a `cell()` outside the grid are all compile errors.

title: 19 ŌĆö The asset pipeline
text:
Every content system ŌĆö models ([`16`](16-modeling.md)), UI ([`21`](21-ui.md)), levels
([`17`](17-levels.md)), tilesets and atlases ([`18`](18-tilemaps.md)), audio ([`22`](22-audio.md)),
fonts ŌĆö shares one shape, specified once here:

> **`source ŌåÆ importer ŌåÆ (content-hashed asset + generated `.gen.fun` seam)`.** The importer is a
> deterministic pure function; the content hash is the asset's identity, cache key, and what makes
> builds incremental; the seam is the typed contract, committed and diffable.

The bake is part of the **`funpack`** binary (pure `source ŌåÆ artifact`,
[`29`](29-architecture-governance.md)), so it is build-deterministic by construction ŌĆö no wall clock,
no network, no machine paths leak into an artifact.

title: 1. One shape for every asset
text:
| Source | Authored as | Importer produces | Seam |
|---|---|---|---|
| `.fpm` model | DSL | mesh + collider, hashed | params `data`, anchors, `MeshHandle` |
| `.fui` screen | DSL | view program | view-model `data`, `Msg` enum, view fn |
| `.flvl` level | DSL | spawn data + tile layers | `Ref` table, `*_spawns`, `TilemapHandle` |
| `.tiles` tileset | DSL | tile defs (cell + collision) | `TilesetHandle` |
| `.atlas` sprite sheet | image + slice spec | atlas (regions + clips) | `AtlasHandle` (+ named cells/clips) |
| audio (WAV/OGG) | raw | decoded buffer | `SoundHandle` |
| font (TTF) | raw | glyph atlas | `Font` |

Two source flavors, one pipeline: **authored DSLs** bake through their importer and emit a seam; **raw
external files** are content-hashed directly through a binary importer. Both end as a content-hashed
asset.

title: 2. Content hash = identity = cache key
text:
```
hash(asset) = H( source bytes ŌŖĢ importer version ŌŖĢ hashes of its dependencies )
```

This one rule buys: **build determinism** (same inputs ŌćÆ same hash ŌćÆ byte-identical artifact
anywhere); **free caching** (the hash is the cache key ŌĆö a bake whose hash is already in the
content-addressed store is skipped, so every build after the first is incremental); and **correct
invalidation** (bumping an importer's version changes the hash of everything it produced ŌĆö a kernel
fix rebuilds exactly its outputs and their dependents, never more). Determinism comes from *caching
the result*, not re-deriving it at runtime.

title: 3. The manifest & the name registry
text:
The bake emits a **manifest** ŌĆö a committed, diffable index mapping each stable asset **name** ŌåÆ
content hash ŌåÆ output location + metadata (the example `assets.manifest`: `[coin] kind=model source=ŌĆ”
importer="model@3" deps=[] hash="sha256:ŌĆ”" out=".cache/ŌĆ”"`). It is the source of truth for resolving
a handle, and its diff is a review surface.

**Asset names are a closed registry, checked at compile time** (the `@gtag`-for-assets, P7): a name
not in the manifest is a **compile error**, so `mesh("krognid_torso")` cannot resolve to nothing at
runtime. The bake also generates a typed **`Assets` seam** of handle constants ŌĆö one per registered
asset, lowercase, namespaced by folder:

```funpack
let coin:     MeshHandle  = MeshHandle{name: "coin"}
let coin_sfx: SoundHandle = SoundHandle{name: "coin_sfx"}
```

The **default, safest** addressing is the typed constant (`assets.coin_sfx`) ŌĆö no string, no possible
typo, propagation holds (rename the source ŌćÆ the constant disappears ŌćÆ readers stop compiling). The
string constructors (`mesh("ŌĆ”")`, `atlas("ŌĆ”")`, `sound("ŌĆ”")`) remain for **dynamic, data-driven**
lookups and are **checked against the manifest**, so even the string form is typo-proof at build
(`assets.coin_sfx == sound("coin_sfx")`).

title: 4. The dependency DAG & importers
text:
Assets form a graph (an atlas depends on its image; a tileset on an atlas; a level on tilesets,
models, and the placed things' schemas). Each output records its input hashes, so a source edit
invalidates only the **dirty subgraph**, the bake is an order-independent, parallelizable walk over a
DAG of pure functions, and a cycle is a build error.

There is **one importer per source kind**, an **engine-closed** battery (not user-authored), each a
deterministic pure function; binary importers (PNG/WAV/TTF/glTF) are Tier-1 native but contracted
deterministic. A genuinely new importer is the **escape hatch** ŌĆö an `extern` importer in
custom-runtime mode ŌĆö never needed on the common path.

**Per-platform texture-compression format is an importer/implementation detail with no authoring
surface** ŌĆö choosing BCn/ASTC/ETC per target is the texture importer's concern, folded into the
content hash via its importer version (┬¦2), and never specified in funpack or named by a handle. A
texture is authored and referenced by name; its on-disk encoding is invisible to game code.

title: 5. The generated seam & where it runs
text:
Every importer yielding funpack-visible types emits a **committed, formatter-canonical,
regenerated-on-bake** `.gen.fun`: it lets the strict world typecheck **before any bake has run**, its
diff is the **review record** for a content change, and a stale committed seam is a build error.

- **`funpack` owns the bake** (pure); the cache is content-addressed and local by default; a
  shared/remote cache is a build-infrastructure concern outside the pure bake.
- **Dev** bakes the dirty subgraph on demand and **hot-reloads** ŌĆö handles resolve through the
  manifest, so swapping an artifact under a stable name updates the running game live; a *type*
  change in a regenerated seam is a recompile, not a hot-swap.
- **Release** bakes everything, then **strips**: **dead-asset elimination** removes any asset no
  handle references (the P5 dead-code gate applied to content). The bake emits an **asset report**
  (counts, sizes, the reference graph, what was stripped ŌĆö the example `assets.report.txt`: "baked 3,
  stripped 1 (unreferenced) ŌĆ” shipped 9 KB across 2 assets"), so truncation is never silent.

**Asset residency is a transparent engine concern behind typed handles.** Streaming and lazy loading ŌĆö
when an artifact is paged into memory and when it is evicted ŌĆö are engine-side scheduling against the
content-addressed store; authoring references a typed handle (`assets.coin`, ┬¦3) and is **unaffected by
residency**. There is no load/unload authoring surface for asset memory, no "is-loaded" query in sim
code: a handle is always a valid reference, and resolving it is the engine's job. (Level *chunk*
streaming ŌĆö `Load`/`Unload` over a `LevelHandle`, [`17`](17-levels.md)#4 ŌĆö is a separate, explicit
gameplay-spawn concern, not asset residency.)

Asset names are namespaced by folder; the manifest records each asset's importer version, and a
release pins those versions.

title: 20 ŌĆö The render pipeline
text:
A render behavior is a pure `fn(self) -> [Draw]` (or `[Draw3]`) over **one committed tick** of
fixed-point state ŌĆö that is what makes it testable by exact equality. Everything a game needs beyond
that (a moving camera, faster-than-tick display, layered draw-lists, post) lives in the **engine**,
operating on the deterministic draw-list the behaviors emit.

> **Thesis:** render behaviors emit a deterministic, fixed-point draw-list of a single committed
> tick; the engine owns camera, frame-rate interpolation, compositing, and post ŌĆö all visual-only,
> off the determinism path. The draw-list is the assertion ground truth; the pixels are display.

This is the fixed-vs-float boundary drawn at the render seam: the sim produces fixed-point *intent to
draw*; the engine produces float *pixels*.

title: 1. Draw commands
text:
**2D** (`engine.render`): `enum Draw { Rect, Line, Text, Fill, Stroke, Sprite, Camera }`.

> `Draw::Camera{ at: Vec2, zoom: Fixed, rotation: Fixed }` (with an optional viewport for
> split-screen) is the 2D twin of `Draw3::Camera`.

`Color` is a closed palette plus `Rgb{ r, g, b }`; `Sprite{ atlas, cell, at, size, tint, flip,
layer }` ([`18`](18-tilemaps.md)); `Fill`/`Stroke` take a `Sketch` ([`16`](16-modeling.md)).

**Anchor (normative):** in every sized draw command (`Rect`, `Sprite`, `Plane`), `at` is the
**center** of the drawn extent, with `size` reaching `size/2` out from it on each axis. A thing's
position is the point its sim logic tests (pong's `overlaps` and `goal_side` read the same `pos`
that `draw_ball` emits), so a draw command anchored at that point renders the entity exactly where
the sim says it is ŌĆö a corner anchor would shift every visual `size/2` off its collision truth. A
backend projecting to a corner-origin pixel space derives the corner at the present boundary
(`at ŌłÆ size/2`); the command itself never carries a corner. `Text` follows the same rule: `at` is
the **center of the rendered glyph run** (the engine renders block text at fixed metrics), so
`Draw::Text{at: board-center}` reads centered without the author knowing the engine's glyph sizes.

**3D** (`engine.render3`): `enum Draw3 { Mesh{handle, at, material}, Rigged{skeleton, parts, pose,
at}, Plane{at, size, color}, Camera{eye, at, fov}, Light{dir, color} }`. `Material{ color, metallic,
rough }` (helpers `matte`/`pbr`); engine PBR, **no user-authored shaders**.

title: 2. Two clocks ŌĆö tick vs. frame
text:
The sim advances at the fixed `tick`; the display refreshes at the monitor rate. **Render runs once
per tick** (pure, fixed-point, exactly what its unit test asserts). The engine **tweens the draw-list
to the display frame**: it holds the tick-N and tick-N+1 draw-lists (the COW version store retains
recent ticks for free, [`08`](08-state.md)) and emits, each frame, an **interpolated** draw-list at
`alpha = accumulator / tick_dt`.

Correlation is automatic: a command is matched across the two ticks by **the id of the producing
thing** (the engine already iterates in stable id order) plus the command's index. For each matched
pair the engine **lerps continuous fields** (`at`, rotation, scale, `tint`/alpha) and **snaps
discrete ones** (sprite `cell`, `text`, mesh handle, `layer`); a despawn culls at the boundary, a
spawn (or a grown list) appears at the boundary. Interpolation is **float and visual-only**: it is
never recorded, synced, or asserted ŌĆö the committed tick states are the deterministic truth.

title: 3. The camera is state; the view is a command
text:
There is no camera subsystem to configure. The camera is **ordinary sim state** ŌĆö a `singleton
Camera` (or a `thing`, for split-screen) ŌĆö driven by **ordinary behaviors**, so follow/deadzone/shake
are pure fixed-point transitions, deterministic and unit-testable. A `view` render behavior emits
`Draw::Camera{ at, zoom, rotation }`; camera-as-data keeps the model uniform and the view interpolates
for free.

- **Logical space, engine letterbox** ŌĆö the game draws in a fixed logical space (Pong's 160├Ś120); the
  engine scales and letterboxes to the window. Sim coordinates never depend on window size. The
  logical space is **declared** in the entrypoint (`logical = WxH`, [`14`](14-project-config.md) ┬¦4)
  ŌĆö the engine letterboxes to the declared extent, never to a constant inferred from game code.
- **WorldŌåöscreen is deterministic** (fixed-point camera), and only the engine reads it; it delivers a
  world-space pointer as an input resource ([`23`](23-input.md)) ŌĆö sim code never sees screen pixels.
- **Multiple camera things ŌåÆ split-screen**, each carrying a viewport rect; the engine renders the
  scene once per camera.

title: 4. Compositing & post
text:
All `render:` `[Draw]` lists concatenate in flattened-pipeline order, then the engine orders the
merged list deterministically:

- **2D depth is the explicit `layer: Int`** ŌĆö a stable sort, ties broken by producing-thing id;
  discrete, so it snaps under interpolation. No implicit list-order depth.
- **3D depth is the camera + z-buffer** (`Draw3::Mesh` depth-tested against the active `Draw3::Camera`).
- **`ui:` composites over `render:` by stage order** ([`21`](21-ui.md)); `audio:` emits no pixels.

**Post-processing** (tonemap, bloom, vignette, grade) is **engine batteries from a closed token set**,
not user shaders ŌĆö float, visual-only, never sim-readable.

title: 5. Determinism
text:
The **draw-list is the deterministic artifact** ŌĆö a render behavior is a pure fixed-point function of
a committed tick, so its `[Draw]`/`[Draw3]` is bit-identical everywhere and is the assertion ground
truth (`screenshot{include_drawlist}` returns it as data, [`28`](28-introspection.md)). **Pixels are
not deterministic and need not be**: interpolation, letterboxing, rasterization, and post are
float/visual, never recorded, synced, or asserted.

title: 6. Scope
text:
The fit policy defaults to letterbox; integer-scale and stretch are presentation config, not a sim
knob. Draw-list correlation is keyed by producing-thing id plus command index (┬¦2).

title: 21 ŌĆö UI (`.fui`)
text:
UI hallucination comes from scattered, hand-authored glue ŌĆö markup, state, dispatch map, and routes
in four places an agent loses the thread across. funpack makes the glue **generated and
type-checked**.

> **Thesis: the binding name *is* the wire.** A `.fui` template ŌĆö HTML-adjacent, single-file ŌĆö
> declares what it *reads* and what it *emits*; the bake materializes **both ends as types** (a
> view-model `data` and a message `enum`); the compiler then forces the logic side to match.

A funpack UI *is* the Elm/React architecture, which is already the functional core: **Model** = a
view-model `data`; **View** = a pure `fn(model) -> View[Msg]`; **Messages** = signals; **Update** =
ordinary behaviors consuming those `Msg` signals. The template exists only to make authoring
single-file and wiring generated.

title: 1. The `.fui` surface
Directives ŌĆö each generates a typed edge
text:
The data-flow triad names a direction of flow per sigil: `:attr=path` (**value in**, Vue bind),
`@event=Msg` (**message out**, Vue event), `bind:value=field` (**both**, Svelte two-way ŌåÆ lowers to a
read *and* a `Set`-message). Structure (`{ }`), control flow (`if`/`for`), and interpolation (`"{x}"`)
are funpack's own, reused ŌĆö a `.fui` is "funpack you can see."

| In the template | Generates |
|---|---|
| `"{score}"`, `if game_over` | a view-model field (`score: Int`, `game_over: Bool`) |
| `:class=tone` | a typed read feeding the attribute (`tone: String`) |
| `@click=Pause` | a `Msg` variant (`Pause`) |
| `@click=SetVolume(p.value)` | a variant carrying the payload (`SetVolume(Int)`) |
| `bind:value=player_name` | **both** a read `player_name: String` *and* a `SetPlayerName(String)` variant |
| `for opt in difficulties { ŌĆ” }` | a list field whose **row type is inferred from the `opt.*` bindings used inside** |

The shapes are **inferred from usage ŌĆö the template *is* the schema.** You never hand-declare the
view-model; you author the view and its data contract falls out. The surface is deliberately **not
JSX** (its power is arbitrary embedded logic ŌĆö exactly what the seam forbids) and **not HTML tags**
(the widget set is closed and non-HTML, and `<>` is banned); it is funpack braces + a thin
declarative-directive layer, so only `@` / `:` / `bind:` are new tokens.

title: 2. The generated seam & anti-hallucination
text:
The bake emits committed funpack ŌĆö the read contract, the write contract, and the screen function:

```funpack
data HudView { score: Int, time_left: Int, game_over: Bool }
enum HudMsg { Coin, Pause, Retry }
extern fn hud(model: HudView) -> View[HudMsg]
```

The game touches only this seam, in two pure pieces ŌĆö a **projection** (`ui:` stage) building the
view-model and an **update** (interior stage) consuming the `Msg`. The payoff is mechanical: an agent
edits **only `hud.fui`**, adds `@click=Mute` ŌåÆ regeneration adds `HudMsg::Mute` ŌåÆ the update's `match`
is non-exhaustive ŌåÆ **compile error naming the exact behavior and missing variant**. Bind a new
`"{combo}"` ŌåÆ `HudView` gains `combo` ŌåÆ the projection no longer constructs ŌåÆ compile error naming
the missing field. The single-file edit **propagates as a typed error that says exactly what the
other domain must do** ŌĆö the generated seam is the firewall keeping each task in one domain.

title: 3. Routing ŌĆö screens are values, navigation is a signal
text:
No route config or string URLs. The set of `.fui` files generates an enum and a tagged message union
(`examples/hud/screens.gen.fun`):

```funpack
enum Screen { Hud, Pause, Settings }
enum AppMsg { Hud(HudMsg), Pause(PauseMsg), Settings(SettingsMsg) }
```

The active screen is sim state; the mount lifts each screen's view into the union with **`View.map`**
(the Elm `Html.map` primitive) ŌĆö `hud(self.hud_view()).map(AppMsg::Hud)` ŌĆö and the update unwraps the
tag and delegates. Adding a `.fui` extends both enums, so the mount *and* the update `match` stop
compiling until the screen is mounted and handled. **The screens *are* the route table**, so routing
cannot drift.

title: 4. The `ui:` stage
text:
`ui:` is a **distinct engine-closed stage** ([`06`](06-things-behaviors.md)), not a flavor of
`render:`: a `render:` behavior is output-only (`[Draw]`), but a `View[Msg]` **declares an inbound
edge** (the messages it can emit), which is categorically not a render behavior.

- A `ui:` behavior is a pure projection `fn(self) -> View[Msg]` with Render's input rules plus the one
  power to mount an interactive tree.
- **Compositing is stage order** ŌĆö `ui:` runs after `render:`, so UI is over the scene by position;
  multiple `ui:` behaviors composite back-to-front in listed order.
- The **`Msg` round-trip is a deferred signal**: the engine hit-tests recorded pointer input against
  the composited tree and delivers each `Msg` next tick (the IO-result class). Effect closure requires
  every mounted `Msg` to be consumed somewhere; convention puts the handler in an early interior
  stage (`input: [on_msg] ŌĆ” ui: [view]`).

title: 5. Determinism & rules
text:
Layout and hit-testing are **deterministic fixed-point** (so "which button did this recorded click
hit" is replay-stable), but layout metrics are **never sim-readable** ŌĆö UI's only sim-observable
output is the hit-tested `Msg` (there is no `width-of` API). UI state is `data` (serializable, part of
the save).

**Transitions & animation are engine visual-only polish off the determinism path.** Fades, slides, and
widget motion are **derived by the engine from view changes and theme tokens** ŌĆö the engine diffs
successive `View`s and animates the delta under theme-supplied timing ŌĆö and carry **no transition state
in the sim**: the projection emits the *target* view, never an in-flight tween. There is **no
imperative UI-animation authoring API** (no `animate(...)`, no timeline, no per-frame callback); like
mixing and device output in audio, animation is engine-side and not sim-observable, so a replay is
identical whether or not a transition was mid-flight.

**Accessibility is an engine rendering concern derived from the closed widget set + semantic theme
tokens.** UI scaling, contrast modes, and screen-reader labels fall out of the same two facts the
rest of UI rests on ŌĆö every widget is one of the closed ~14 kinds (so the engine knows its role and can
expose a semantic label) and every style token is a semantic theme role (`bg-panel`, `text-2xl`, so the
engine can rescale or recontrast without touching templates). There is **no separate accessibility
authoring surface** ŌĆö no aria attributes, no alt-text directive, no contrast flag in the `.fui`; an
author writes the closed widgets and semantic tokens, and accessibility is a rendering derivation
([`20`](20-render.md)), settings-driven like other per-machine preferences ([`24`](24-persistence.md)).

- **Expression sublanguage: paths and literals only** ŌĆö no operators, calls, or ternaries; the one
  computation is string interpolation. Anything computed is a *named* view-model field set in the
  projection (so it is unit-testable): not `{score * 2}` but a field `doubled`; not `:class={sel ?
  ŌĆ”}` but `tone: String`.
- **Focus & gamepad** ŌĆö focus is engine-managed deterministic state (a cursor over focusable widgets
  in document order, `focus-N` override). It is driven by the closed `UiAction` set
  (`stdlib/engine/ui.fun`: `NavUp`/`NavDown`/`NavLeft`/`NavRight`/`Confirm`/`Cancel`), bound in the
  *same* `bindings` function as gameplay input; **`Confirm` on a focused widget emits that widget's
  `@click` Msg**, so pointer, keyboard, and gamepad all reach one author-written binding.
- **List identity** ŌĆö positional by default; `for item in items key=item.id` for stable identity on
  reorder/insert/delete (a `Ref[T]` is the natural key).
- **Payload typing** ŌĆö an event payload is a path; primitives infer, a domain type is named once with
  an inline ascription (`for opt in difficulties: { id: Ref[Difficulty] } { ŌĆ” }`).
- **Enum variant as a function value** (a general language fact surfaced by `.map`): `AppMsg::Hud`
  denotes `fn(HudMsg) -> AppMsg`; a nullary variant is the value itself.

**Incremental replacement:** the seam (`HudView`, `HudMsg`, `fn hud`) is the `@stub`-for-UI analog ŌĆö
a `.fui` template can be replaced by a hand-authored `engine.ui`-builder view (`panel`/`text`/
`button`/`class`/`when`/`map`) behind the same seam (`backing: template("hud.fui")` ŌåÆ
`funpack(hud_view)`), with no change to the projection or update.

title: 22 ŌĆö Audio
text:
Sound is an **effect**, and funpack effects are **data returned to the engine** (commands-as-data).
There is no `play_sound()` ŌĆö a behavior returns audio *values* and the engine produces the sound, so
audio is pure, testable, and replay-deterministic like everything else. It splits into the two
regimes the rest of the engine uses:

| Regime | Surface | Trigger | Returned from |
|---|---|---|---|
| **One-shot SFX** | `[Sound]` command | edge (an event happened) | an Update behavior, alongside other commands |
| **Sustained audio** | `[Audio]` scene | level (this *should* be playing now) | the `audio:` stage, diffed by the engine |

title: 1. One-shot ŌĆö `[Sound]`
2. Sustained ŌĆö the `audio:` stage
3. The `audio:` stage contract
text:
`audio:` is an engine-closed stage kind alongside Render/Ui: a pure projection `fn(self) -> [Audio]`
with Render's input rules (blackboard / resources / `View`; no signal lists, no `Rng`, no writes). It
is **output-only** ŌĆö unlike Ui it has no inbound edge, because sound emits nothing back. Stage order
among `render:` / `ui:` / `audio:` is irrelevant; they are independent projections.

title: 4. Buses & determinism
text:
A `Bus` (`Master / Music / Sfx / Ui / Voice`) groups sounds for volume control; a settings slider
drives a bus by feeding its value into the gain of the sounds on it ŌĆö no mutable global mixer.
**Triggers are deterministic data** (a replay re-emits the identical sequence), and **output never
feeds back**: mixing, latency, and device output are engine-side and not sim-observable (like `Draw`).
`gain`/`pitch` are `Fixed` (they often derive from sim, e.g. pitch from speed), but the sim never
reads anything *back* from audio. Positional audio attenuates relative to the listener, which defaults
to the active `Draw3::Camera`; a `Sound`/`Audio` carries an optional world `at`.

Per-bus gain is folded into the projection ŌĆö there is no global mixer to mutate. The listener
defaults to the active `Draw3::Camera`.
